perturbation.original_text: Upon either party’s request, each party hereto shall (and shall cause its Representatives, and any other person, to whom it has disclosed any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information) promptly either (at the Receiving Party’s option) return to the other party hereto or destroy (and certify in writing to the other party by an authorized officer supervising such destruction) all copies or other reproductions of Proprietary 2 Information of the other party, other than any Derivative Materials, in its possession or the possession of any of its Representatives, and shall not retain any copies or other reproductions, in whole or in part, of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon either party’s request, each party hereto shall (and shall cause its Representatives, and any other person, to whom it has disclosed any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information) return to the other party hereto or destroy (and certify in writing to the other party by an authorized officer supervising such destruction) all copies or other reproductions of Proprietary Information of the other party, other than any Derivative Materials, in its possession or the possession of any of its Representatives within 90 days of the request, and shall not retain any copies or other reproductions, in whole or in part, of such materials.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires prompt return or destruction of proprietary information. Modifying it to allow a 90-day window contradicts the concept of 'prompt' action, which is not consistent with regulations requiring immediate action in certain data protection contexts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Personal Information Security Breach Notification Act
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires businesses to notify affected individuals and the state attorney general of a security breach involving private information 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay.' Allowing a 90-day delay in returning or destroying data after a request hinders prompt action required by the law, especially if a breach has occurred.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires prompt action regarding proprietary information, whereas the changed text introduces a 90-day delay. The Law Explanation states that New York law requires businesses to notify individuals of security breaches 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay'. While the provided scraped snippets don't directly specify a timeframe for returning or destroying data, the delay introduced by the modified text directly contradicts the 'most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' standard described in the Law Explanation, especially if a breach has occurred. This inconsistency between the requirement for prompt action and the allowed 90-day delay establishes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the Receiving Party (or any of its Representatives) shall be Legally Required to disclose any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information, the Receiving Party shall, in advance of such disclosure, to the extent legally permissible, provide the Disclosing Party with notice of such requirement and a description of the Proprietary Information and Transaction Information that the Receiving Party or its Representative intends to disclose and to reasonably cooperate with the Disclosing Party to the extent it may seek to limit such disclosure, including, if requested, taking all reasonable steps, at the Disclosing Party’s expense, to resist or avoid any such legal, judicial, regulatory or administrative process or to obtain a protective order in respect thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the Receiving Party (or any of its Representatives) shall be Legally Required to disclose any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information, the Receiving Party shall, within one business day of such disclosure, provide the Disclosing Party with notice of such requirement and a description of the Proprietary Information and Transaction Information that the Receiving Party or its Representative intends to disclose and will not cooperate with the Disclosing Party to limit such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires advance notice and cooperation. The modified text provides only one business day notice *after* the disclosure and no cooperation to limit it, which violates regulations requiring reasonable attempts to protect confidential information before disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities and Exchange Commission Regulation S-P
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Regulation S-P requires financial institutions to adopt policies and procedures to protect customer information. While the regulation doesn't explicitly forbid disclosure under legal requirements, the lack of advance notice or cooperation to limit disclosure goes against the spirit of protecting confidential financial information, especially where the Receiving Party might have options to limit the scope of disclosure.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 248.30 - Procedures to safeguard customer information, including response programs for unauthorized access to customer information and customer notice; disposal of customer information and consumer information. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 248.30 - Procedures to safeguard customer information, including response programs for unauthorized access to customer information and customer notice; disposal of customer information and consumer information. CFR Table of Popular Names § 248.30 Procedures to safeguard customer information, including response programs for unauthorized access to customer information and customer notice; disposal of customer information and consumer information. (a) Policies and procedures to safeguard customer information —(1) General requirements. Every covered institution must develop, implement, and maintain written policies and procedures that address administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for the protection of customer information. (2) Objectives. These written policies and procedures must be reasonably designed to: (i) Ensure the security and confidentiality of customer information; (ii) Protect against any anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of customer information; and (iii) Protect against unauthorized access to or use of customer information that could result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any customer . (3) Response programs for unauthorized access to or use of customer information. Written policies and procedures in paragraph (a)(1) of this section must include a program reasonably designed to detect, respond to, and recover from unauthorized access to or use of customer information, including customer notification procedures . This response program must include procedures for the covered institution to: (i) Assess the nature and scope of any incident involving unauthorized acc
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts SEC Regulation S-P, specifically concerning the safeguarding of customer information. The original text mandates advance notice and cooperation with the disclosing party when the receiving party is legally required to disclose proprietary information. The changed text alters this to provide notice only 'within one business day of such disclosure' and explicitly states the receiving party 'will not cooperate' in limiting disclosure. This directly undermines the intent of 17 CFR § 248.30, which requires covered institutions to 'develop, implement, and maintain written policies and procedures that address administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for the protection of customer information' and ensure 'the security and confidentiality of customer information'. By eliminating advance notice and cooperation, the changed text weakens the safeguards designed to protect confidential information, creating a clear contradiction with the regulatory requirement to protect customer data.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of being furnished the Proprietary Information by the other party, each party agrees that, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement, such party and its Restricted Affiliates shall not employ or solicit to employ any of the current officers or employees of the other party or its subsidiaries with whom such party or its Representatives has had contact in connection with the evaluation of the Possible Transaction or who were specifically identified to such party during the period of investigation of the other party pursuant to this Agreement, without obtaining the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of being furnished the Proprietary Information by the other party, each party agrees that, for a period of twenty-four (24) months from the date of this Agreement, such party and its Restricted Affiliates shall not employ or solicit to employ any of the current officers or employees of the other party or its subsidiaries, regardless of whether such employees had any contact with the party or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: The original text included a restriction on soliciting or employing employees with whom there was contact related to the transaction. The modified text broadens the restriction to *any* employee and extends the period to 24 months. This potentially creates an unreasonable restraint on trade and could be challenged under state laws prohibiting overly broad non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The modified clause, which broadly restricts the employment of *any* employee for 24 months, is likely unenforceable in California because it's too broad and not narrowly tailored to protect legitimate business interests, potentially violating the statute's prohibition on restraints of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract broadens the restriction on employing employees of the other party to *any* employee for a period of 24 months, regardless of contact. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The modified clause, by restricting the employment of any employee for 24 months, creates a restraint of trade that is likely unenforceable under California law, as it is not narrowly tailored to protect legitimate business interests, as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of New York and will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of Switzerland and will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies New York law. Switching the governing law to Switzerland could conflict with the application of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 to certain transactions involving companies listed on US exchanges or having significant US operations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as enforced by the SEC, has jurisdiction over securities transactions in the United States. While parties can generally choose governing law, a choice-of-law provision selecting Swiss law might be disregarded if it is determined that applying Swiss law would undermine the policies and protections of US securities laws, particularly if the transaction has significant connections to the United States (e.g., one of the companies is listed on a US exchange). Section 78aa grants jurisdiction to US district courts over violations of the Act.

perturbation.justification: The change from New York law to Swiss law as the governing law in the contract creates a potential contradiction with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The Law Explanation states that choosing Swiss law might be disregarded if it undermines US securities laws, especially if the transaction has significant US connections. Scraped Law Snippet 2 directly mentions the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and its associated USC Title and Section, indicating its applicability. The change could allow parties to evade US securities regulations if the transaction involves US-listed companies or substantial US operations.
